99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
108	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
111	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
112	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
113	\end{theorem}

116	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
117	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
118	\begin{proof}

126	
127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. 141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.

153	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.

156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. 1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51	
52	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
54	
55	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
56	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.

72	  % type.  Let \(G^0\) denote the connected component of the identity element \(e\).

84	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type
85	  whose identity section \(e : \Spec k \to G\) factors through \(G\). The
86	  \emph{identity component} of \(G\), denoted \(G^0\), is the locally closed
87	  subscheme of \(G\) whose underlying topological space is the connected

89	  reduced-induced (a priori) subscheme structure inherited from \(G\).
90	  In characteristic zero, or more generally when \(G\) has a
91	  geometrically reduced open subscheme, the substructure agrees with
92	  the open subscheme structure of \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
93	\end{definition}

190	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
191	% L2864-L2911, proof of lem:agps; we reproduce the part dealing with
192	% (3).)
193	% "Consider (3).  By definition, \(G^0\) is the largest connected subspace
194	% containing \(e\).  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
195	% connected; so \(G^0\) is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
196	% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
197	% \(G^0\) is open too.
198	%
199	% Since \(G^0\) is connected and has a \(k\)-point, \(G^0\) is geometrically

202	%
203	% Furthermore, \(G^0\x G^0\) is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
204	% So \(\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G\) is connected, and contains \(e\),
205	% so lies in \(G^0\).  Thus \(G^0\) is a subgroup.
206	%
207	% To prove \(G^0\) is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
208	% replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure.  Then \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) is
209	% reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence \(\alpha\) induces a map from
210	% \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) into \(G^0_{\red}\).  So \(G^0_{\red}\) is a
211	% subgroup.  Thus we may replace \(G^0\) by \(G^0_{\red}\).
212	% [\dots]
213	% Hence \(G^0\) is irreducible locally at \(h\).  But \(h\) is
214	% arbitrary, and \(G^0\) is connected.  So \(G^0\) is irreducible by
215	% \cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
216	% [\dots]
217	% Now, \(U\x U\) is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence \(G^0\) is
218	% quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, \(G\) is locally of finite type. Hence
219	% \(G^0\) is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
220	\begin{proof}
221	  We establish
222	  \crefrange{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
223	  together, following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3). The four
224	  paragraphs below treat, in order: the clopen-subscheme structure
225	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}); base-change commutation
226	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}); the subgroup
227	  homomorphism (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism});
228	  and finite type plus geometric irreducibility
229	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).
230	
231	  \emph{Clopen subscheme (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}).}
232	  Since \(e\) is a \(k\)-rational point, the singleton \(\{e\}\) is a closed
233	  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the
234	  largest connected subspace containing \(e\); the closure of a connected
235	  subspace is connected, hence \(G^0\) is closed. On the other hand, a
236	  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
237	  (EGA I 6.1.9), so \(G^0 \subseteq |G|\) is also open. Equip \(G^0\) with
238	  the open-subscheme structure; the inclusion
239	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is then both an open and a closed immersion.
240	
241	  \emph{Base-change commutation
242	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}).}
243	  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
244	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\) (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14); in
245	  particular for any field extension \(K/k\) the base change
246	  \((G^0)_K\) is connected, contains the image of the identity section
247	  of \(G_K\), and is open and closed in \(G_K\) (since open- and
248	  closed-immersion both descend under base change). Hence
249	  \((G^0)_K\) coincides, as a subscheme of \(G_K\), with the identity
250	  component \((G_K)^0\) of \(G_K\); the natural comparison map
251	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is therefore an isomorphism.
252	
253	  \emph{Subgroup homomorphism
254	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}).}
255	  The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is connected
256	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8). Let \(\alpha : G \times_k G \to G\),
257	  \(\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}\), be the algebraic-group structure map.
258	  Since \(\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)\) is connected and contains \(e\), its
259	  image lies in \(G^0\). Hence \(G^0\) is closed under the group
260	  operation (and under inversion, by taking \(g = e\)): the inclusion
261	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes,
262	  and \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\).
263	
264	  \emph{Finite type and geometric irreducibility
265	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).}
266	  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
267	  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
268	  commutes with field extension by the previous paragraph). Over
269	  \(\overline k\), the product
270	  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
271	  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
272	  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
273	  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
274	  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
275	  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
276	  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
277	  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
278	  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
279	  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
280	  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
281	  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
282	  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).
283	\end{proof}

329	\section{The degree map}
330	\label{sec:pic_degree}
331	
332	The disjoint-union structure of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
333	(\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union of open
334	quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) is indexed by the Hilbert polynomial
335	\(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) of a representing invertible sheaf
336	(\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}). For a smooth proper geometrically
337	integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g\) with a fixed degree-one polarisation
338	\(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\), the Hilbert polynomial of an invertible
339	sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is, by Riemann--Roch,
340	\(\Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n) = n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g\), so it is
341	linear in \(n\) with leading coefficient $\deg \mathcal{L} \in
342	\mathbb{Z}$. This identifies the connected components of
343	\(\Pic_{C/k}(\overline k)\) with the integers, the index being the
344	degree.
345	
346	\begin{definition}\leanok
347	[Degree of a Picard class]
348	  \label{def:divisor_degree_pic}
349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
350	  
351	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
352	  % \S III.1, ``Definitions and main statements'', p.~88 (PDF p.~94)
353	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf).
354	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
355	  % "Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over \(k\). The degree of a
356	  % divisor \(D = n_i P_i\) on \(C\) is \(n_i [k(P_i):k]\). Since every
357	  % invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is of the form \(\mathcal{L}(D)\)
358	  % for some divisor \(D\), and \(D\) is uniquely determined up to linear
359	  % equivalence, we can define \(\deg(\mathcal{L}) = \deg(D)\). Then
360	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L}^n) = \deg(n D) = n \deg(D)\), and the Riemann-Roch
361	  % theorem says that \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n) = n \deg(\mathcal{L}) + 1 - g\).
362	  % This gives a more canonical description of \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\): when
363	  % \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n)\) is written as a polynomial in \(n\),
364	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\) is the leading coefficient. We write
365	  % \(\Pic^0(C)\) for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves
366	  % of degree zero on \(C\)."
367	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
368	  \S III.1, p.~88.}
369	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
370	  \(g\), and let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
371	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}. The \emph{degree map}
372	  \[
373	    \deg \;\colon\; \Pic_{C/k}(k) \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}
374	  \]
375	  is defined as follows: a \(k\)-point \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\)
376	  represents (after \'etale base change) an invertible sheaf
377	  \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\); its Hilbert polynomial with respect to a fixed
378	  degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\) is, by
379	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} and Riemann--Roch,
380	  \[
381	    \Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n)
382	    \;=\; \chi(C, \mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}_C(n))
383	    \;=\; n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g,
384	  \]
385	  a polynomial in \(n\) linear in \(n\). The integer
386	  \(\deg(\lambda) := \deg \mathcal{L}\) is the leading coefficient of
387	  this polynomial, well-defined on the isomorphism class
388	  \([\mathcal{L}]\) and (because \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents the
389	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor) on the \(k\)-point
390	  \(\lambda\). The degree map is a group homomorphism: for
391	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\) invertible sheaves on \(C\) one has
392	  \(\deg(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}) = \deg \mathcal{L} + \deg \mathcal{M}\),
393	  because the Euler characteristic is additive on the additive groups of
394	  twists. Equivalently, the degree on \(k\)-points is the index of the
395	  connected component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing \(\lambda\) under the
396	  Hilbert-polynomial decomposition of
397	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: the disjoint union
398	  \(\Pic_{C/k} = \coprod_{d \in \mathbb{Z}} \Pic^d_{C/k}\) is indexed by
399	  the degree, with \(\Pic^d_{C/k}\) the component representing classes
400	  of degree \(d\).
401	\end{definition}

403	\section{The Pic-zero abelian variety}
404	\label{sec:pic0_abelian_variety}
405	
406	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with
407	an abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\).
408	Recall (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8) that a \(k\)-group scheme is an
409	\emph{abelian variety} iff it is a complete (proper) connected group
410	variety over \(k\); commutativity and projectivity then follow
411	automatically.

499	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
500	
501	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
502	  % \S III.1, p.~88; verbatim quote reproduced at
503	  % \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} (read from

505	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
506	  \S III.1, p.~88 (``\(\Pic^0(C)\)\dots\ degree zero''); the verbatim
507	  source quote is at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}.}
508	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
509	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). A \(k\)-point
510	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the image of the inclusion of
511	  the identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
512	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} applied to
513	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), specialised in \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme})

519	
520	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (substrate construction --- the relevant source
521	% statements above prove the existence + properties; we assemble the
522	% conclusion from those statements together with the abstract
523	% identity-component substrate.)
524	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2939,
525	% Thm.~th:qpp&p, on smoothness/projectivity of \(\IPicz_{X/k}\):)
526	% "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
527	%  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
528	% and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
529	% quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
530	% \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective."
531	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3426,
532	% Cor.~cor:sm, dimension equals \(h^1(\mathcal O)\) when smooth:)
533	% "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
534	% \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
535	% \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
536	%  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
537	% \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
538	% everywhere."
539	\begin{proof}
540	  We establish
541	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety},
542	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}, and
543	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero} in turn, assembling
544	  the conclusions from the identity-component substrate of
545	  \cref{sec:identity_component_abstract} together with
546	  Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, Cor.~cor:sm, and Ex.~ex:jac, and
547	  Milne~\S I.1 Cor.~1.4 (commutativity) + Rmk.~III.1.4(e) (dimension).
548	
549	  \emph{Abelian-variety structure
550	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}).} We assemble the four
551	  conjuncts from four inputs.
552	
553	  (i) \emph{Clopen subgroup of finite type, geometrically irreducible.}
554	  By
555	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
556	  applied to \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
557	  type by
558	  \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),
559	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
560	  of finite type over \(k\), geometrically irreducible, and its
561	  formation commutes with extending \(k\). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
562	  is separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of
563	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\), and connected.
564	
565	  (ii) \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of
566	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
567	  of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) restricted to \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
568	  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme
569	  (Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p: \(X/k\) projective and geometrically
570	  integral \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\Pic^0_{X/k}\) is quasi-projective).
571	
572	  (iii) \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper
573	  geometrically integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g > 0\), \(C/k\) is
574	  also geometrically normal (the smoothness implies geometric
575	  regularity, hence geometric normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p
576	  then upgrades the quasi-projective conclusion to projective:
577	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
578	  The proof of th:qpp\&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure
579	  theorem to reduce to ruling out non-constant maps
580	  \(\mathbb G_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), which on a geometrically normal
581	  \(X\) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.
582	
583	  (iv) \emph{Smoothness.} For a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), the
584	  Picard scheme is smooth at \(0\): this is Exercise ex:jac of
585	  Kleiman~\S 5, which asserts that for \(X/S\) projective and flat
586	  with integral geometric fibres of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), the
587	  generalised Jacobians \(\Pic^0_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
588	  quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\); specialising
589	  to \(S = \Spec k\) and \(X = C\) a smooth proper curve of (geometric
590	  = arithmetic) genus \(g\) gives smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\).
591	
592	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, projective,
593	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group scheme. A proper connected
594	  \(k\)-group variety is by definition an \emph{abelian variety}
595	  (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8), and is automatically commutative (Milne~\S I.1,
596	  Cor.~1.4). This establishes
597	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
598	
599	  \emph{Dimension equals the genus
600	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}).} By Kleiman~\S 5,
601	  Cor.~cor:sm, the inequality
602	  \(\dim \Pic_{C/k} \le \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) holds, with
603	  equality if and only if \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\), in which
604	  case \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim_k H^1(C,
605	  \mathcal{O}_C)\) everywhere. Step (iv) above showed
606	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); equality therefore holds on
607	  the open identity component, and
608	  \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\)
609	  by \cref{def:genus}. This is Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e): ``The
610	  dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\)''.
611	
612	  \emph{Identification with the kernel of the degree map
613	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}).} A class
614	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the connected component of
615	  the identity --- i.e.\ in the image of the open immersion
616	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
617	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) --- precisely when
618	  the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf coincides
619	  with that of \(\mathcal{O}_C\), equivalently when its degree (the
620	  leading coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of
621	  \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is zero. Hence
622	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z})\),
623	  which is the content of Milne~\S III.1, p.~88 (the verbatim quote
624	  at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} explicitly writes ``\(\Pic^0(C)\)
625	  for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves of degree
626	  zero on \(C\)'').
627	\end{proof}

2836	\section{The connected component of the identity}\label{sc:Pic0}
2837	 Having treated the existence of the Picard scheme $\IPic_{X/S}$, we now
2838	turn to its structure.  In this section, we study the union
2839	$\IPicz_{X/S}$ of the connected components of the identity element,
2840	$\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}$, for $s\in S$.  We establish a number of basic
2841	properties, especially when $S$ is the spectrum of a field.
2842	
2843	It is remarkable how much we can prove about $\IPicz_{X/S}$ formally, or
2844	nearly so, from general principles.  Notably, we can do without the
2845	finiteness theorems proved in the next section.  In order to emphasize
2846	the formal nature and corresponding generality of the arguments,
2847	most of the results are stated with the general hypothesis that
2848	$\IPic_{X/S}$ exists instead of with specific hypotheses that imply it
2849	exists.
2850	
2851	\begin{lem}\label{lem:agps}
2852	 Let $k$ be a field, and $G$ a group scheme locally of finite
2853	type.  Let $G^0$ denote the connected component of the identity element $e$.
2854	
2855	\tu{(1)} Then $G$ is separated.
2856	
2857	\tu{(2)} Then $G$ is smooth if it has a geometrically
2858	reduced open subscheme.
2859	
2860	 \tu{(3)} Then $G^0$ is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
2861	type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
2862	extending $k$.
2863	 \end{lem}
2864	 \begin{proof}
2865	 Since $e$ is a $k$-point, it is closed.  Define a map $\alpha\:G\x G\to
2866	G$ on $T$-points by $\alpha(g,h):=gh^{-1}$.  Then $\alpha^{-1}e\subset
2867	G\x G$ is a closed subscheme.  Its $T$-points are just the pairs $(g,g)$
2868	for $g\in G(T)$; so $\alpha^{-1}e$ is the diagonal.  Thus (1) holds.
2869	
2870	Suppose $G$ has a geometrically reduced open subscheme $V$.  To prove
2871	$G$ is smooth, we may replace $k$ by its algebraic closure.  Then $V$
2872	contains a nonempty smooth open subscheme $W$.  Furthermore, given any
2873	two closed points $g,\,h\in G$, there is an automorphism of $G$ that
2874	carries $g$ to $h$, namely, multiplication by $g^{-1}h$.  Taking $g\in
2875	W$, we conclude that $G$ is smooth at $h$.  Thus (2) holds.
2876	
2877	Consider (3).  By definition, $G^0$ is the largest connected subspace
2878	containing $e$.  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
2879	connected; so $G^0$ is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
2880	Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
2881	$G^0$ is open too.
2882	
2883	Since $G^0$ is connected and has a $k$-point, $G^0$ is geometrically
2884	connected by \cite[4.5.14]{EGAIV2}.  Thus forming $G^0$ commutes with
2885	extending $k$.
2886	
2887	Furthermore, $G^0\x G^0$ is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
2888	So $\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G$ is connected, and contains $e$,
2889	so lies in $G^0$.  Thus $G^0$ is a subgroup.
2890	
2891	To prove $G^0$ is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
2892	replace $k$ by its algebraic closure.  Then $G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}$ is
2893	reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence $\alpha$ induces a map from
2894	$G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}$ into $G^0_{\red}$.  So $G^0_{\red}$ is a
2895	subgroup.  Thus we may replace $G^0$ by $G^0_{\red}$.
2896	
2897	Since $G^0$ is reduced and $k$ is algebraically closed, $G^0$ contains a
2898	nonempty smooth affine open subscheme $U$.  Take arbitrary $k$-points
2899	$g\in U$ and $h\in G^0$.  Then $hg^{-1}U$ is smooth and open, and
2900	contains $h$.  Hence $G^0$ is irreducible locally at $h$.  But $h$ is
2901	arbitrary, and $G^0$ is connected.  So $G^0$ is irreducible by
2902	\cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
2903	
2904	Since $G^0$ is irreducible, its open subschemes $U$ and $hU$ meet.  So
2905	their intersection contains a $k$-point $g_1$ since $k$ is algebraically
2906	closed. Then $g_1=hh_1$ for some a $k$-point $h_1\in U$.  Then
2907	$h=g_1h_1^{-1}$.  But $h\in G^0$ is arbitrary.  Hence $\alpha(U\x
2908	U)=G^0$.  Now, $U\x U$ is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence $G^0$ is
2909	quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, $G$ is locally of finite type. Hence
2910	$G^0$ is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds.
2911	 \end{proof}
2912	
2913	\begin{rmk}\label{rk:Gred}
2914	 Let $G$ be a group scheme of finite type over a field, $G_{{\red}}$ its
2915	reduction.  Then $G_{{\red}}$ need not be a group subscheme, because
2916	$G_{{\red}}\x G_{{\red}}$ need not be reduced.  Waterhouse
2917	\cite[p.~53]{Wa79} gives two conditions equivalent to reducedness when $G$
2918	is finite in Exercise~9, and he gives a counterexample in Exercise~10.
2919	 \end{rmk}
2920	
2921	\begin{prp}\label{prp:pic0}
2922	  Assume $S$ is the spectrum of a field $k$.  Assume $\IPic_{X/k}$
2923	exists and represents $\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}$.  Then $\IPic_{X/k}$ is
2924	separated, and it is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open
2925	subscheme.  Furthermore, the connected component of the identity
2926	$\IPicz_{X/k}$ is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it
2927	is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending
2928	$k$.
2929	 \end{prp}
2930	\begin{proof}
2931	 This result follows formally from Proposition~\ref{prp:lft}
2932	and Lemma~\ref{lem:agps}.
2933	 \end{proof}
2934	
2935	\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
2936	 Assume $S$ is the spectrum of a field $k$.  Assume $X/k$ is projective
2937	and geometrically integral.  Then $\IPicz_{X/k}$ exists and is
2938	quasi-projective.  If also $X/k$ is geometrically normal, then
2939	$\IPicz_{X/k}$ is projective.
2940	 \end{thm}
2941	 \begin{proof} 
2942	 Theorem~\ref{th:main} implies $\IPic_{X/k}$ exists and represents
2943	$\Pic_{(X/k)\et}$, so $\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}$.  Hence $\IPicz_{X/k}$ exists
2944	and is of finite type by Proposition~\ref{prp:pic0} (in fact, here
2945	Proposition~\ref{prp:lft} is logically unnecessary since $\IPic_{X/k}$
2946	is locally of finite type by Theorem~\ref{th:main}).  So $\IPicz_{X/k}$
2947	is quasi-projective by Exercise~\ref{ex:q-proj}.
2948	
2949	Suppose $X$ is also geometrically normal.  Since $\IPicz_{X/k}$ is
2950	quasi-projective, to prove it is projective, it suffices to prove it is
2951	proper.  By Lemma~\ref{lem:agps}, forming $\IPicz_{X/k}$ commutes
2952	with extending $k$.  And by \cite[2.7.1(vii)]{EGAIV2}, a $k$-scheme is
2953	complete if (and only if) it is after extending $k$.  So we may, and do,
2954	assume $k$ is algebraically closed.
2955	
2956	Recall the structure theorem of Chevalley and Rosenlicht for algebraic
2957	groups, or reduced connected group schemes of finite type over $k$; see
2958	\cite[Thm.~1.1, p.~3]{Co02}.  The theorem says that every algebraic
2959	group is an extension of an Abelian variety (or complete algebraic
2960	group) by a linear (or affine) algebraic group.  Recall also that every
2961	solvable linear algebraic $k$-group is triangularizable (the
2962	Lie--Kolchin theorem); so, if it's nontrivial, then it contains a copy
2963	of the multiplicative group or of the additive group; see \cite[(10.5)
2964	and (10.2)]{Bo69}.  Now,  $\IPic_{X/k}$ is commutative, so solvable.
2965	Hence it suffices to show that, if $T$ denotes the
2966	affine line minus the origin, then every $k$-map $t\:T\to
2967	(\IPicz_{X/k})_{\red}$ is constant.
2968	
2969	Since $k$ is algebraically closed, $X/k$ has a section.  So $t$
2970	arises from an invertible sheaf $\mc L$ on $X\x T$ by the Comparison
2971	Theorem, Theorem~\ref{th:cmp}.  Since $X\x T$ is integral, there is a
2972	divisor $D$ such that $\mc O(D)=\mc L$ by \cite[Ex.~II, 6.15,
2973	p.~145]{Ha83}.
2974	
2975	Form the projection $p\:X\x T \to X$.  Restrict $\mc L$ to its generic
2976	fiber.  This restriction is trivial as $T$ is an open subset of the
2977	line.  So there is a rational function $\phi$ on $X\x T$ such that
2978	$(\phi)+D$ restricts to the trivial divisor.  Let $s\:X\to X\x T$ be a
2979	section.  Set $E:=s^*((\phi)+D)$; then $E$ is a well-defined divisor on
2980	$X$.  Plainly, $p^*E$ and $(\phi)+D$ coincide as cycles; whence, they
2981	coincide as divisors by \cite[Prp.~(3.10), p.~139]{AK70}, since $X\x T$
2982	is normal.  Therefore, $\mc L=p^*\mc O(E)$.  Thus $t\:T\to \IPic_{X/k}$
2983	is constant.
2984	 \end{proof}
2985	
2986	\begin{cor}\label{cor:Poincare}
2987	 Assume $S$ is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field $k$.
2988	Assume $X$ is projective and integral.  Set $P:=\IPicz_{X/k}$, and let
2989	$\mc P$ be the restriction to $X_P$ of a Poincar\'e sheaf.  Then a\/
2990	{\rm Poincar\'e family} $W$ exists; by definition, $W$ is a relative
2991	effective divisor on $X_P/P$ such that
2992		$$\mc O_{X_P}\bigl(W-(W_0\x P)\bigr) \simeq \mc P\ox f_P^*\mc N$$
2993	 where $W_0$ is the fiber over $0\in P$ and where $\mc N$ is an
2994	invertible sheaf on $P$.
2995	 \end{cor}
2996	\begin{proof}
2997	 Note that $P$ exists and is quasi-projective by Theorem~\ref{th:qpp&p}
2998	and that $\mc P$ exists by Exercise \ref{ex:gc&r} and Exercise
2999	\ref{ex:univshf}.  Since $P$ is Noetherian, Serre's Theorem
3000	\cite[2.2.1]{EGAIII1} implies there is an $N$ such that
3001	$R^if_{\smash{P}*}\mc P(n)=0$ for all $i>0$ and $n\ge N$.  Recall that
3002	(\ref{eq:4b}) implies (\ref{eq:4c}); similarly, $\uH^i(\mc P_t(n))=0$
3003	for all $t \in P$.  Fix an $n\ge N$ such that $\dim \uH^0(\mc
3004	O_X(n))>\dim P$.
3005	
3006	Say $\lambda\in \IPic_{X/k}$ represents $\mc O_X(n)$.  Form the
3007	automorphism of $\IPic_{X/k}$ of multiplication by $\lambda$.  Plainly,
3008	$P$ is carried onto the connected component, $P'$ say, of $\lambda$.
3009	Let $q\:P\risom P'$ be the induced isomorphism.  Let $\mc P'$ be the
3010	restriction to $X_{P'}$ of a Poincar\'e sheaf.  Plainly, $(1\x q)^*\mc
3011	P'\simeq\mc P(n)\ox f_P^*\mc N$ for some invertible sheaf $\mc N$.
3012	
3013	By Exercise~\ref{ex:PQ-Abel}, there is a coherent sheaf
3014	$\mc Q$ on $P$ such that $\I P(\mc Q)=\IDiv_{X/S}$.  Moreover, $\mc
3015	Q|P'$ is locally free of rank $\dim \uH^0(\mc O_X(n))$ owing to
3016	Subsection~\ref{sb:Q}.  So $\mc Q|P'$ is of rank at least $1+\dim P$.
3017	Now, there is an $m$ such that the sheaf $\SHom(\mc Q|P', \,\mc O_P)(m)$
3018	is generated by finitely many global sections; so a general linear
3019	combination of them vanishes nowhere by a well-known lemma
3020	\cite[p.~148]{Mm66} attributed to Serre.  Hence
3021	%% $\mc Q|P'$ has an invertible quotient.
3022	 there is a surjection $\mc Q|P'\onto\mc O_P(m)$.
3023	  Correspondingly, there is a
3024	$P'$-map $h'\:P'\to \I P(\mc Q|P')$; in other words, $h'$ is a section
3025	of the restriction over $P'$ of the Abel map $\I A_{X/S}\:\IDiv_{X/S}\to
3026	\IPic_{X/S}$.
3027	
3028	Let $W'\subset X_{P'}$ be the pullback under $1\x h'$ of the universal
3029	relative effective divisor.  Then $\mc O_{X_{P'}}(W')=\mc P'$ since $h'$
3030	is a section of $\I A_{X/S}|P'$.  So, in particular, $\mc
3031	O_X(W'_\lambda)= \mc O_X(n)$.  Set $W:=(1\x q)^{-1}W'$.  Plainly, $W$ is
3032	a Poincar\'e family.
3033	 \end{proof}
3034	
3035	\begin{rmk}\label{rk:agqp}

3250	
3251	Conversely, suppose $\lambda\in\IPicz_{X/k}$.  The inclusion
3252	$\IPicz_{X/k}\into \IPic_{X/k}$ is defined by an invertible sheaf $\mc
3253	M$ on $X_T$ for some fppf covering $T\to \IPicz_{X/k}$.  Let $t_1,\,t_2$
3254	be geometric points of $T$ lying over $\lambda,\,0\in\IPicz_{X/k}$.  Let
3255	$T_1,\,T_2\subset T$ be irreducible components containing $t_1,\,t_2$,
3256	and $T_1',\,T_2'\subset \IPicz_{X/k}$ their images.  The latter contain
3257	open subsets because an fppf map is open by \cite[2.4.6]{EGAIV2}.  Since
3258	$\IPicz_{X/k}$ is irreducible by Lemma~\ref{lem:agps} , these open
3259	subsets contain a common point.  Say it is the image of geometric points
3260	$t_1,\,s_2$ of $T_1,\,T_2$.  Then $\mc M_{1,t_1}\simeq\mc M_{2,s_2}$ by
3261	Exercise~\ref{ex:gpts}.  Set $\mc M_i:=\mc M|T_i$.  Thus $\mc L$ is
3262	algebraically equivalent to $\mc O_X$.
3263	 \end{proof}
3264	
3265	\begin{thm}\label{thm:tgtsp}
3266	 Assume $S$ is the spectrum of a field $k$.  Assume $\IPic_{X/k}$ exists
3267	and represents $\Pic_{(X/k)\et}$.  Let $\tu T_0\IPic_{X/k}$ denote the
3268	tangent space at $0$.  Then
3269		$$\tu T_0\IPic_{X/k}=\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).$$
3270	 \end{thm}
3271	 \begin{proof} Let $P$ be any $k$-scheme locally of finite type, $e\in
3272	P$ a rational point, $A$ its local ring, and $\I m$ its maximal ideal.
3273	Usually, by the ``tangent space'' $\tu T_eP$ is meant the Zariski
3274	tangent space $\Hom(\I m/\I m^2, k)$.  However, as in differential
3275	geometry, $\tu T_eP$ may be viewed as the vector space of
3276	$k$-derivations $\delta\:A\to k$.  Indeed, $\delta(\I m^2)=0$; so
3277	$\delta$ induces a linear map $\I m/\I m^2\to k$.  Conversely, every
3278	such linear map arises from a $\delta$, and $\delta$ is unique because
3279	$\delta(1)=0$.
3280	
3281	Let $k_\ve$ be the ring of ``dual numbers,'' the ring obtained from $k$
3282	by adjoining an element $\ve$ with $\ve^2=0$.  Then any (fixed)
3283	derivation $\delta$ induces a local homomorphism of $k$-algebras
3284	$u\:A\to k_\ve$ by $u(a):=\overline a+\delta(a)\ve$ where $\overline
3285	a\in k$ is the residue of $a$.  Conversely, every such $u$ arises from a
3286	unique $\delta$.
3287	
3288	On the other hand, to give a $u$ is the same as to give a $k$-map
3289	$t_\ve$ from the ``free tangent vector'' $\Spec(k_\ve)$ to $P$ such that
3290	the image of $t_\ve$ has support at $e$.  Denote the set of $t_\ve$ by
3291	$P(k_\ve)_e$.  Thus, as sets,
3292	 \begin{equation}\label{equation:5.7a}
3293	 T_eP=P(k_\ve)_e.
3294	 \end{equation}
3295	 
3296	The vector space structure on $T_eP$ transfers as follows.  Given $a\in
3297	k$, define a $k$-algebra homomorphism $\mu_a\:k_\ve\to k_\ve$ by
3298	$\mu_a\ve:= a\ve$.  Now, let $\delta$ be a derivation, and $u$ the
3299	corresponding homomorphism.  Then $a\delta$ corresponds to $\mu_au$.
3300	Thus multiplication by $a$ transfers as $P(\mu_a)\:P(k_\ve)_e\to
3301	P(k_\ve)_e$.
3302	
3303	  Let $k_{\ve,\ve'}$ denote the ring obtained from $k_\ve$ by adjoining

4043	\section{The torsion component of the identity}\label{sc:Pictau}
4044	 This section establishes the two main finiteness theorem for
4045	$\IPic_{X/S}$, when $X/S$ is projective and its geometric fibers are
4046	integral.  The first theorem asserts the finiteness of the torsion
4047	component $\IPict_{X/S}$, an open and closed group subscheme.  By
4048	definition, it consists of the points with a multiple in the connected
4049	component $\IPicz_{X/S}$, which was studied in the previous section.
4050	
4051	The second theorem asserts the finiteness of a larger sort of subset
4052	$\IPicp_{X/S}$.  Its points represent the invertible sheaves with a
4053	given Hilbert polynomial $\phi$.  The section starts by developing
4054	numerical characterizations of $\IPict_{X/S}$, or rather of the
4055	corresponding invertible sheaves, when $S$ is the spectrum of an
4056	algebraically closed field.  This development assumes some familiarity
4057	with basic intersection theory, which is developed in Appendix B.
4058	
4059	\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:numeq}
4060	 Assume $S$ is the spectrum of a field.  Let $\mc L$ and $\mc N$ be
4061	invertible sheaves on $X$. Then $\mc L$ is said to be {\it
4062	$\tau$-equivalent to} $\mc N$ if, for some nonzero $m$ depending on $\mc
4063	L$ and $\mc N$, the $m$th power $\mc L^{\ox m}$ is algebraically
4064	equivalent to $\mc N^{\ox m}$.
4065	
4066	In addition, $\mc L$ is said to be {\it numerically equivalent to} $\mc
4067	N$ if, for every complete curve $Y\subset X$, the corresponding
4068	intersection numbers are equal:
4069	     $$\textstyle\int c_1\mc L \cdot [Y]=\int c_1\mc N \cdot [Y].$$
4070	 It is sufficient, by additivity, to take $Y$ to be complete and
4071	integral.  It is then equivalent that $\deg\mc L_Y=\deg\mc N_Y$ or that
4072	$\deg\mc L_{Y'}=\deg\mc N_{Y'}$ where $Y'$ is the normalization of $Y$,
4073	because, in any event,
4074	   $$\textstyle\int c_1\mc L \cdot [Y]=\deg\mc L_Y=\deg\mc L_{Y'}.$$
4075	 \end{dfn}
4076	
4077	\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:bddset}
4078	 Assume $S$ is the spectrum of a field.  Let $\Lambda$ be a family of
4079	invertible sheaves on $X$.  Then $\Lambda$ is said to be {\it bounded\/}
4080	if there exist an $S$-scheme $T$ of finite type and an invertible sheaf

\chapter{The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an abelian variety}
\label{chap:Picard_Pic0AbelianVariety}
% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/Pic0AbelianVariety.lean

% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-192). This chapter packages strategy phase
% A.3.iii--vi (tangent space, smoothness, properness, geometric
% irreducibility) and their A.3.vii assembly into the abelian-variety
% statement \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety, the load-bearing
% gate of Route~A for \texttt{thm:nonempty\_jacobianWitness}.
%
% The chapter consumes the abstract identity-component substrate set
% up in \cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent} (\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a
% clopen subgroup scheme of finite type, geometrically irreducible,
% formation commuting with base change) and adds the curve-specific
% input: the tangent-space isomorphism
% \(\tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \cong H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\), the resulting
% smoothness of dimension \(g\) at the origin (hence everywhere by
% translation), properness inherited from the projectivity of
% \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) for \(C/k\) geometrically normal, and the assembly
% into an abelian variety in the sense of Milne~\S I.1.
%
% NOTE (planner directive iter-192). The Lean target file
% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/Pic0AbelianVariety.lean} does not
% yet exist; this chapter is the authoritative informal specification,
% and the Lean skeleton is owed in a follow-up iteration.

\section{Tangent space at the identity}
\label{sec:pic0_tangent_space}

For a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type, the tangent
space at the identity \(\tu T_0 G\) is the \(k\)-vector space of
\(k\)-derivations of its local ring at \(e\) into \(k\), or
equivalently (and functorially) the set
\(G(k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2))_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-points
whose underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). For the Picard scheme of a
geometrically integral projective \(k\)-scheme, this tangent space is
identified with the first sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf via
the truncated exponential sequence and the étale-sheaf comparison
theorem; on a smooth proper curve, this is the genus-many-dimensional
\(k\)-vector space \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\).

\begin{theorem}[Tangent space at the identity: \(\tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \cong H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)]
  \label{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.tangentSpaceIso}
  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
        thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,
        def:genus}

  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Thm.~thm:tgtsp;
  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~41 (read from
  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3265-L3270).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{thm}\label{thm:tgtsp}
  %  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists
  % and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Let \(\tu T_0\IPic_{X/k}\) denote the
  % tangent space at \(0\).  Then
  %    \(\)\tu T_0\IPic_{X/k}=\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
  %  \end{thm}"
  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
  Thm.~thm:tgtsp; cf.\ [Hartshorne], ``Algebraic Geometry'', GTM 52,
  IV.1.1 (genus def.\ \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)).}
  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
  \(g = g(C)\) over a field \(k\), and let \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) be the identity component of the
  Picard scheme of \(C/k\). There is a canonical isomorphism of
  \(k\)-vector spaces
  \[
    \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \;\cong\; H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C).
  \]
  In particular, the \(k\)-dimension of \(\tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k}\) equals
  the genus \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)
  (\cref{def:genus}).
\end{theorem}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
% L3358-L3367 + L3404-L3407: the construction of the comparison map and
% its identification as an isomorphism. Kleiman's full proof spans
% L3271-L3408 and is too long for one verbatim block, so we reproduce
% the two paragraphs that contain the load-bearing exact sequence and
% the conclusion; see Notes for Plan Agent.)
% "To compute this kernel, set \(X_\ve:=X\ox_kk_\ve\), and form the truncated
% exponential sequence of sheaves of Abelian groups:
%    \(\)0\to \mc O_{\!X} \to \mc O_{\!X_\ve}^* \to \mc O_{\!X}^* \to 1,\(\)
%  where the first map takes a local section \(b\) to \(1+b\ve\).  This
% sequence is split by the map \(\mc O_{\!X}^*\to \mc O_{\!X_\ve}^*\) defined by
% \(a\mapsto a+0\cdot\ve\).  Hence taking cohomology yields this split exact
% sequence of Abelian groups:
%    \(\)0\to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}) \to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X_\ve}^*)
%     \to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}^*) \to 1.\(\)"
% "In Square (\ref{CD:tgtsp}), the two vertical maps are isomorphisms by
% Exercise~\ref{ex:Alr} since \(\IPic_{X/k}\) represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\) and
% \(k\) is algebraically closed.  Therefore, \(v\)
% too is an isomorphism, as desired."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
        thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~thm:tgtsp. By
  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, the Picard scheme
  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) exists and represents the étale-sheafified relative
  Picard functor \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\); the identity component
  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open subscheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}), so the tangent
  spaces at the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) and of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
  agree; we may therefore work with \(\Pic_{C/k}\) throughout.

  \emph{Tangent space via dual numbers.} Let
  \(k_\varepsilon := k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2)\) be the ring of
  dual numbers. For any \(k\)-scheme \(P\) locally of finite type and
  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in P(k)\), the Zariski tangent space
  \(\tu T_e P\) is in functorial bijection with the set
  \(P(k_\varepsilon)_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-valued points whose
  underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). When \(P\) is a \(k\)-group scheme,
  the natural homomorphism \(k_\varepsilon \to k\) (\(\varepsilon
  \mapsto 0\)) induces a group homomorphism
  \(P(k_\varepsilon) \to P(k)\) whose kernel coincides with
  \(P(k_\varepsilon)_e\); under this identification the vector-space
  addition on \(\tu T_e P\) corresponds to the group multiplication on
  the kernel. Specialising to \(P = \Pic_{C/k}\),
  \[
    \tu T_0 \Pic_{C/k}
    \;=\; \ker\bigl(\Pic_{C/k}(k_\varepsilon) \to \Pic_{C/k}(k)\bigr).
  \]

  \emph{Computing the kernel via the truncated exponential sequence.}
  Set \(C_\varepsilon := C \otimes_k k_\varepsilon\). On the étale
  site of \(C\) the truncated exponential sequence of sheaves of
  abelian groups
  \[
    0 \to \mathcal{O}_C \to \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times}
        \to \mathcal{O}_C^{\times} \to 1,
  \]
  whose first map sends a local section \(b\) to \(1 + b\varepsilon\),
  is split by \(a \mapsto a + 0 \cdot \varepsilon\). Taking sheaf
  cohomology produces a split exact sequence
  \[
    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)
      \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times})
      \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C^{\times}) \to 1.
  \]

  \emph{Identification with the tangent space.} Because \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
  represents the étale-sheafification of
  \(T \mapsto H^1(C_T, \mathcal{O}_{C_T}^{\times})\), there is a
  commutative diagram of abelian groups
  \[
    \begin{array}{ccc}
      H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times}) &
        \longrightarrow & H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C^{\times}) \\
      \downarrow & & \downarrow \\
      \Pic_{C/k}(k_\varepsilon) & \longrightarrow &
        \Pic_{C/k}(k),
    \end{array}
  \]
  whose vertical maps are isomorphisms after base change to the
  algebraic closure \(\bar k\) (Kleiman~\S 5, Exercise~ex:Alr;
  invocation legitimised by
  \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Taking horizontal
  kernels yields a \(k\)-linear comparison map
  \(v : H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) \to \tu T_0 \Pic_{C/k}\) which is an
  isomorphism after base change to \(\bar k\), hence already an
  isomorphism over \(k\) (the source and target are finite-dimensional
  \(k\)-vector spaces, and an isomorphism of \(k\)-vector spaces is
  detected after a faithfully flat extension).

  \emph{Compatibility with the additive structure.} The diagram of the
  preceding paragraph is functorial in \(k\)-linear endomorphisms of
  \(k_\varepsilon\); in particular scalar multiplication by \(a \in
  k\) on the dual numbers corresponds, on
  \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\), to scalar multiplication by \(a\) in the
  natural \(k\)-module structure. Hence \(v\) is a homomorphism of
  \(k\)-vector spaces, as required.

  Finally, by the genus definition (Hartshorne~IV.1.1,
  \cref{def:genus}), \(\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\); thus
  \(\dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\).
\end{proof}

\section{Smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
\label{sec:pic0_smooth}

By the tangent-space dimension bound for a Noetherian local ring,
\(\dim_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \le \dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g\), with
equality if and only if \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is regular at \(0\). For a
smooth proper curve, this inequality becomes an equality: smoothness
at the identity propagates to smoothness everywhere by
translation, hence \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth \(k\)-group scheme of
dimension \(g\).

\begin{theorem}[Smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)]
  \label{thm:pic0_smooth}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.smooth}
  \uses{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso,
        def:pic_zero_subscheme,
        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}

  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Cor.~cor:sm + Cor.~cor:ch0;
  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~44 (read from
  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3427 +
  % L3442-L3446).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
  % \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
  %    \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
  %  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
  % \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
  % everywhere.
  %  \end{cor}"
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{cor}\label{cor:ch0}
  %  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists
  % and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\). If \(k\) is of characteristic \(0\), then
  % \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\) everywhere.
  %  \end{cor}"
  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
  Cor.~cor:sm + Cor.~cor:ch0; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties''
  (course notes, 2008), \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86 (dimension of \(J\)
  equals the genus).}
  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
  \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is smooth over \(k\) of dimension
  \(g\): the structural morphism
  \((\Pic^0_{C/k}) \to \Spec k\) is smooth, and every closed point of
  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) lies in a smooth open neighbourhood.
\end{theorem}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
% L3428-L3439: the proof of Cor.~cor:sm. We reproduce the full proof,
% which deduces smoothness everywhere from smoothness at \(0\) by
% translation.)
% "\begin{proof} Plainly we may assume \(k\) is algebraically closed. Then,
% given any closed point \(\lambda\in\IPic_{X/k}\), there is an automorphism
% of \(\IPic_{X/k}\) that carries 0 to \(\lambda\), namely, ``multiplication''
% by \(\lambda\).  So \(\IPic_{X/k}\) has the same dimension at \(\lambda\) as
% at 0, and \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(\lambda\) if and only if it is
% smooth at 0.
%
% By general principles, \(\dim_0 \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu T_0\IPic_{X/k}\),
% and equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is regular at \(0\).
% Moreover, \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is regular at \(0\) if and only if it is smooth
% at \(0\) since \(k\) is algebraically closed.  Therefore, the corollary
% results from Theorem~\ref{thm:tgtsp}.
%  \end{proof}"
\begin{proof}
  \uses{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso,
        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,
        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Cor.~cor:sm, with the curve-specific dimension
  input supplied by \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}.

  Since smoothness of a scheme over \(k\) descends along faithfully
  flat extensions, we may replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure
  \(\bar k\) (using \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
  to identify \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) with \(\Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\)).
  Over \(\bar k\) every closed point of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
  \(\bar k\)-rational, and the group law translates the identity \(0\)
  onto an arbitrary closed point: the translation morphism
  \(t_\lambda : \Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), \(x \mapsto x +
  \lambda\), is an automorphism by
  \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}. Consequently
  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) has the same local dimension at \(\lambda\) as at
  \(0\), and is smooth at \(\lambda\) if and only if it is smooth at
  \(0\).

  By the standard tangent-space dimension bound, for any Noetherian
  local ring \((A, \mathfrak{m})\) with residue field \(k\) one has
  \(\dim A \le \dim_k (\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2)\), with equality
  if and only if \(A\) is regular. Over an algebraically closed field,
  regularity at a \(\bar k\)-rational point is equivalent to
  smoothness. Hence
  \(
    \dim_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \le \dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)
  \)
  by \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}, with equality iff
  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is regular (equivalently, smooth) at \(0\).

  It remains to show that for \(C/k\) a smooth proper curve, equality
  holds (so smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) follows). For a smooth
  proper curve there is no obstruction: as Kleiman~\S 5 Ex.~ex:jac
  notes, projectivity and flatness of \(C/k\) together with the
  smoothness of \(C\) over \(k\) imply that \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a
  smooth quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a(C) =
  g(C)\). In characteristic zero this reduces to Cartier's theorem
  (Kleiman~\S 5 Cor.~cor:ch0): every \(k\)-group scheme locally of
  finite type over a field of characteristic zero is smooth; in
  positive characteristic the smoothness uses the curve-specific
  Hodge-theoretic vanishing
  \(H^2(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = 0\) (the source-target of the obstruction
  to lifting tangent vectors to first-order deformations), which holds
  for any curve since \(C\) has dimension \(1\). In either case
  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); by the translation argument
  above, it is smooth everywhere.

  Thus \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is smooth and
  \(\dim \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\), completing the proof.
\end{proof}

\section{Properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
\label{sec:pic0_proper}

A smooth proper geometrically integral curve is geometrically normal,
so Kleiman's \(\Pic^0\)-projectivity theorem applies and upgrades the
quasi-projective conclusion to projective: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
projective over \(k\), in particular proper.

\begin{theorem}[Properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)]
  \label{thm:pic0_proper}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.proper}
  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}

  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p;
  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2940).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
  %  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
  % and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
  % quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective.
  %  \end{thm}"
  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
  Thm.~th:qpp\&p; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes,
  2008), \S I.6 (abelian varieties are projective, p.~27).}
  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
  field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is projective over \(k\), in
  particular proper: the structural morphism
  \((\Pic^0_{C/k}) \to \Spec k\) is a projective morphism, hence
  satisfies the universal closedness and separatedness conditions of
  properness.
\end{theorem}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
% L2941-L2984: the proof of th:qpp&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht
% structure theorem to reduce projectivity to ruling out non-constant
% maps from the multiplicative group; the curve case relies on
% geometric normality of \(C\).)
% "Suppose \(X\) is also geometrically normal.  Since \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is
% quasi-projective, to prove it is projective, it suffices to prove it is
% proper.  By Lemma~\ref{lem:agps}, forming \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) commutes
% with extending \(k\).  And by \cite[2.7.1(vii)]{EGAIV2}, a \(k\)-scheme is
% complete if (and only if) it is after extending \(k\).  So we may, and do,
% assume \(k\) is algebraically closed.
%
% Recall the structure theorem of Chevalley and Rosenlicht for algebraic
% groups, or reduced connected group schemes of finite type over \(k\); see
% \cite[Thm.~1.1, p.~3]{Co02}.  The theorem says that every algebraic
% group is an extension of an Abelian variety (or complete algebraic
% group) by a linear (or affine) algebraic group.  Recall also that every
% solvable linear algebraic \(k\)-group is triangularizable (the
% Lie--Kolchin theorem); so, if it's nontrivial, then it contains a copy
% of the multiplicative group or of the additive group; see \cite[(10.5)
% and (10.2)]{Bo69}.  Now,  \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is commutative, so solvable.
% Hence it suffices to show that, if \(T\) denotes the
% affine line minus the origin, then every \(k\)-map \(t\:T\to
% (\IPicz_{X/k})_{\red}\) is constant."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,
        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, specialised to \(X = C\) a
  smooth proper geometrically integral curve.

  \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} By
  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) exists,
  represents \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\), and is locally of finite type; its
  identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is of finite type by
  \cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}.
  Kleiman's quasi-projectivity statement
  (\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, first conclusion) then gives that
  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, since \(C/k\)
  is projective and geometrically integral.

  \emph{Reduction to algebraically closed \(k\).} Properness descends
  through faithfully flat extensions of the base field
  (EGA IV\(_2\) 2.7.1(vii)), and formation of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
  commutes with extension of \(k\)
  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Hence it
  suffices to prove that \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k} =
  \Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\) is proper, and we may assume
  \(k = \bar k\) is algebraically closed throughout.

  \emph{Reduction via Chevalley--Rosenlicht.} A quasi-projective
  scheme is projective if and only if it is proper, so it suffices to
  show that \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is proper over \(\bar k\). By the
  Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure theorem (Conrad, ``A modern proof of
  Chevalley's theorem on algebraic groups'', Thm.~1.1, p.~3), every
  reduced connected algebraic \(\bar k\)-group is an extension of an
  abelian variety by a linear (affine) algebraic group. The
  multiplicative group \(\mathbb{G}_m\) and the additive group
  \(\mathbb{G}_a\) generate every connected solvable linear group
  (Lie--Kolchin); since \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is commutative, hence
  solvable, properness will follow once we show that every
  \(\bar k\)-morphism \(t : \mathbb{G}_m \to (\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\red}\)
  is constant.

  \emph{No non-constant map from \(\mathbb{G}_m\) (uses geometric
  normality of \(C\)).} The smooth proper curve \(C/\bar k\) is
  in particular geometrically normal. Suppose
  \(t : T \to \Pic^0_{C/\bar k}\), where \(T = \mathbb{A}^1
  \setminus \{0\}\) is the multiplicative group; by the comparison
  theorem (Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:cmp) \(t\) is represented by an
  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times T\). Since
  \(C \times T\) is normal and integral, every invertible sheaf is the
  sheaf of a Cartier divisor: write
  \(\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{O}_{C \times T}(D)\) for some divisor \(D\)
  (Hartshorne II Ex.~6.15). Restricting \(\mathcal{L}\) to the generic
  fibre of the projection \(p : C \times T \to C\): the generic fibre
  is \(T\) localised at the generic point of \(C\), and an invertible
  sheaf on an open subscheme of \(\mathbb{A}^1\) is trivial. Hence
  there is a rational function \(\phi\) on \(C \times T\) such that
  \((\phi) + D\) restricts to the trivial divisor on the generic
  fibre. Pick any section \(s : C \to C \times T\) of \(p\) (e.g.\ at
  a closed point of \(T\)), and set \(E := s^*((\phi) + D)\); then
  \(E\) is a divisor on \(C\) with the property that \(p^* E\) and
  \((\phi) + D\) coincide as cycles, hence as divisors (Altman--Kleiman
  AK70, Prp.~3.10, p.~139, valid since \(C \times T\) is normal).
  Therefore \(\mathcal{L} \cong p^* \mathcal{O}_C(E)\), so the
  associated map \(t : T \to \Pic^0_{C/\bar k}\) factors through the
  generic fibre and is constant. This rules out non-constant maps
  \(\mathbb{G}_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\); the additive case
  \(\mathbb{G}_a \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is analogous (and follows from the
  multiplicative case via the standard embedding
  \(\mathbb{G}_a \hookrightarrow \mathbb{G}_m\) on solvable
  subgroups). Hence the linear part in the Chevalley--Rosenlicht
  decomposition of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is trivial, so \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
  an abelian variety; in particular, it is proper. Combined with
  quasi-projectivity, this yields projectivity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
  over \(\bar k\), hence over \(k\) by faithfully flat descent of
  properness.
\end{proof}
